items.myth: Any count data should be modeled with Poisson.
items.reality: Poisson fits equidispersed non-negative integer counts with independent events at constant rate. Over-dispersion, excess zeros, or bounded counts need other models.
items.example: Hospital length-of-stay counts with many zeros and long tails often fit zero-inflated Negative Binomial better than Poisson(λ).

items.myth: Gaussian and Normal are different distributions used in different contexts.
items.reality: They are the same family N(μ, σ²); “Gaussian” and “Normal” are interchangeable names.
items.example: Gaussian NB in scikit-learn implements the same Normal PDF formulas taught as the Normal distribution.

items.myth: Softmax outputs are arbitrary scores, not a distribution.
items.reality: Softmax produces π_k ≥ 0 with Σ π_k = 1, exactly parameterizing a Categorical distribution for the next label.
items.example: Logits [2, 1, 0] → π ≈ [0.665, 0.245, 0.090]; `-log π_y` for true class y is categorical cross-entropy.

items.myth: Bernoulli and Binomial are unrelated to deep learning losses.
items.reality: Binary cross-entropy is negative Bernoulli log-likelihood; categorical cross-entropy is negative Categorical log-likelihood.
items.example: For label y=1 and predicted p=0.8, loss −[y log p + (1−y) log(1−p)] = −log(0.8), the Bernoulli NLL.
